questions.question: What is the difference between `iter()`, `into_iter()`, and `iter_mut()`?
options: iter() borrows, into_iter() takes ownership, iter_mut() borrows mutably
questions.explanation: `iter()` yields &T, `iter_mut()` yields &mut T, `into_iter()` yields T (consumes the collection).

questions.question: Iterators in Rust are lazy — they do nothing until consumed.
questions.explanation: Adaptors like `.map()` and `.filter()` build a chain. A consumer like `.collect()` or `.sum()` drives it.

questions.question: Consume the iterator to collect results into a Vec:
questions.code:
let nums = vec![1, 2, 3];
let doubled: Vec<i32> = nums.iter().map(|x| x * 2).___();
questions.explanation: `.collect()` consumes the iterator and gathers results. The type annotation guides inference.

questions.question: Which method keeps only elements matching a predicate?
questions.explanation: `.filter(|x| predicate)` yields only items where the closure returns true.

questions.question: Sum all elements of this iterator:
questions.explanation: `.sum()` consumes the iterator and adds up all elements.

questions.question: Iterator is a trait, and you can implement it for your own types.
questions.explanation: Implement `Iterator` by defining `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>`. All adaptor methods come free.
